title: The Insurance Weapon: How Hormuz Closed Without a Blockade
subhead: Iran didn't need to mine the Strait of Hormuz — it let the insurance market do it instead. The same playbook now threatens Europe's Baltic and Black Sea energy corridors, while Ukraine demonstrates that drone warfare can reach deep into Russian oil infrastructure.

sections.heading: Hormuz: The Insurance Cascade That Closed a Chokepoint
sections.body:
On the eve of the US-Israeli strikes on Iran, 56 tankers sailed through the Strait of Hormuz. Two days later, Lloyd's List counted just seven tankers and a single gas carrier — a collapse of over 80% before Iran laid a single mine. The strait was priced shut by insurance markets, not blocked by naval forces.

Major marine war-risk insurers terminated existing cover within 48 hours of the conflict's opening. The Joint War Committee expanded the high-risk zone designation, triggering cancellation clauses in standard policies. Shipping companies that cannot sail uninsured simply self-deterred. This is the new mechanism of chokepoint closure: credible threat plus rational market behavior equals effective blockade at near-zero military cost.

Iran has now formalized this control by creating the Persian Gulf Strait Authority — a government agency to vet and tax ships seeking passage. Secretary Rubio called it 'unacceptable,' but the question remains: what can the world do about it? Iran has demonstrated that a critical waterway carrying roughly 20% of global oil and LNG can be closed not by mining or naval force, but by drone threats and insurance repricing.

The cost asymmetry is staggering. A Shahed drone costs roughly $10,000. A single LNG cargo entering the Baltic is worth $40-80 million. Iran closed Hormuz with threats and insurance premiums. Russia has studied this playbook carefully.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the West would be establishing sovereign reinsurance facilities and permanent war-risk monitoring cells before the next chokepoint crisis. The trap is believing that conventional naval presence prevents commercial closure — it doesn't. The insurance market does not ask whether a navy can stop the next attack, but whether anyone can guarantee there won't be one. Without state backstops for strategic shipping, the market unilaterally severs supply chains.

sections.heading: US Escalates Tanker War as Ceasefire Frays
sections.body:
US forces fired on and disabled two Iranian oil tankers on Friday after exchanging fire with Iranian forces in the Strait of Hormuz overnight. The UAE reported another Iranian missile and drone attack, with three people wounded. The attacks cast further doubt on a month-old ceasefire that Washington insists is still in effect.

The US is now fighting a two-front blockade war — Iran blocking the Gulf, the US blocking Iranian ports. Iran has mostly closed the critical waterway for global energy since February 28; the US has imposed its own counter-blockade. Daily transits remain in single digits, with hundreds of vessels bottled up. A Chinese-crewed tanker was attacked near the strait — China continues importing Iranian oil despite the closure, highlighting Beijing's willingness to absorb risk for energy security while publicly expressing concern.

Iran's Foreign Ministry condemned US actions as violating the ceasefire, noting that 'every time a diplomatic solution is on the table, the US opts for a reckless military adventure.' The contradiction between Washington's insistence that the ceasefire holds and the daily exchange of fire reveals a fundamental reality: the conflict has entered a zone of neither war nor peace, where escalation happens through maritime interdiction rather than territorial conquest.
sections.whyItMatters: The strategic stakes have shifted from whether the US can reopen Hormuz to whether it can prevent Iran from institutionalizing chokepoint control. Iran's creation of a formal transit authority suggests it intends to normalize this leverage. The decisive move would be Washington building a genuine multinational coalition for commercial escort — not just naval presence but guaranteed insurance and coordinated protection. The trap is believing that military escalation alone will restore the status quo ante; Iran has already changed the structural reality.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Deep-Strike Campaign Against Russian Energy
sections.body:
Ukraine struck the Lukoil-Permnefteorgsintez refinery in Perm for the third time in two weeks, hitting a facility over 1,500 kilometers from its borders. The Security Service of Ukraine (SBU) also targeted the Perm linear production dispatch station — a strategic hub in Russia's oil transportation network distributing oil in four directions. One of the facility's oil reservoirs was hit.

Ukraine has institutionalized deep-strike warfare against Russian energy infrastructure, demonstrating that Russia's vast territorial depth provides no sanctuary. Since early 2026, Ukraine has intensified attacks on refineries, oil terminals, and pumping stations as part of a systematic campaign to increase the economic costs of war and force Moscow to divert resources toward domestic infrastructure defense.

Russian authorities report Ukraine is deploying an additional 25,000 unmanned ground vehicles for front-line combat — a parallel droneification of ground warfare. But the strategic innovation is in the rear: Ukraine has shown that a non-state-aligned actor can systematically target the energy infrastructure of a nuclear power at continental depth. The Perm refinery is one of Russia's largest, supplying both civilian and military fuel needs.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Russia would be to accept that air defense over energy infrastructure cannot be 100% effective and to harden dispersal and redundancy instead. The trap is over-investing in point defense when the attacker can always find gaps. For Ukraine's partners, the lesson is that economic pressure through infrastructure strikes is now a standard feature of modern warfare — one they may face themselves.

sections.heading: Europe's Energy Exposure: The Baltic and Black Sea Playbooks
sections.body:
Russia has already weaponized the Black Sea grain corridor, and the Hormuz precedent offers a template for potential Baltic disruption. The insurance-cascade mechanism that closed Hormuz can travel to European waters — not by mining the Danish Straits, but by making them uninsurable.

Northern Europe's post-2022 energy security depends on LNG delivered by sea. Germany built Baltic-side terminals at Mukran, Poland expanded Świnoujście, Lithuania commissioned Klaipėda, Finland added Inkoo. Every cargo destined for these terminals enters the Baltic through one of three passages: the Øresund, Great Belt, or Little Belt. The geography is narrow, the trade concentrated, and the insurance market is the same global system that shut down Hormuz in 48 hours.

Russia produces Shahed-type drones at industrial scale — 3,000 units per month at Alabuga — with ranges exceeding 1,300 kilometers. While there is no open-source confirmation of these systems currently based at Kaliningrad, the range envelope from the exclave and forward positions in Belarus comfortably covers the Danish Straits. Russian Gerbera drones have already entered Lithuanian and Polish airspace multiple times. In September 2025, unidentified drones forced the closure of Danish airports at Copenhagen and Aalborg, directly adjacent to the straits.

Poland's LNG supply, like Finland's and the Baltic states', runs exclusively through the Danish Straits. A disruption there would not reroute supply — it would sever it.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for NATO would be permanent war-risk monitoring cells with Lloyd's Joint War Committee, standby sovereign reinsurance for strategic cargoes, and persistent counter-drone capabilities at chokepoints. The trap is assuming NATO's presence makes these waters safe — it doesn't. Insurance repricing happens before consensus among 32 allies can form. The drone does not need to hit the tanker; it needs to hit the premium.

sections.heading: Türkiye's LNG Pivot: Positioning as Europe's Energy Bypass
sections.body:
Türkiye announced it could sign a larger, longer LNG contract with Algeria — potentially increasing volumes from 4.4 bcm to 6-6.5 bcm annually — as President Tebboune visited Ankara. Energy Minister Bayraktar noted that 'Europe cannot offer Algeria a long-term commitment, but we can,' signaling Türkiye's intent to position itself as a stable energy partner for Mediterranean suppliers seeking alternatives to Hormuz-disrupted Gulf shipments.

Türkiye is leveraging its geographic position to become an LNG gateway to southeastern Europe, offering what Brussels cannot: long-term contract certainty. Ankara is discussing a project where Algerian LNG shipped to Türkiye could be regasified at Turkish facilities and partially exported onward to Europe via Bulgaria. The country's state energy company TPAO is also negotiating joint oil and gas exploration with Algeria's Sonatrach in Algerian waters.

For Türkiye, the Hormuz closure is an opportunity to demonstrate its value as an alternative energy corridor. The country sits outside the Hormuz disruption, maintains working relationships with both Western and regional actors, and has invested heavily in regasification infrastructure. The Algerian deal would extend Türkiye's energy leverage while providing Sofia and other southeastern European capitals with supply security they cannot obtain from Brussels.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's decisive move is positioning itself as the non-disrupted alternative for Mediterranean energy trade — not competing with Gulf supplies but replacing them for European customers. The trap is overcommitting to LNG infrastructure that could become stranded if Hormuz reopens and Gulf prices normalize. The geopolitical value lies in Türkiye's ability to offer what the EU structurally cannot: rapid long-term commitments without 27-member consensus.

sections.heading: The Global LNG Shock: IEA Warns of Structural Supply Loss
sections.body:
The International Energy Agency reported that the Iran war has already eliminated approximately 120 billion cubic metres of global LNG supply from the 2026-2030 outlook — a structural hit to medium-term gas markets. Edison expects Qatar to restart LNG supply 30-45 days after any peace agreement, but the timelines for market normalization remain uncertain.

Qatar's LNG exports, representing roughly 20% of global trade, have been effectively halted by the Hormuz closure. The IEA assessment confirms what the insurance cascade already demonstrated: the market is pricing in a protracted disruption, not a temporary shock. Taiwan estimates it has secured enough gas through September, the latest effort by the chipmaking hub to boost energy resilience.

The supply loss is asymmetric in its effects. Asia faces higher diesel prices accelerating China's shift to electric trucks. Europe faces a structural question about whether its post-2022 LNG import architecture — built rapidly to replace Russian pipeline gas — has simply substituted one vulnerability for another. The Danish Straits analysis is particularly pointed: Europe built Baltic LNG terminals whose supply must pass through waters that Russia could render uninsurable.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for importers would be accelerating diversification toward Mediterranean and Atlantic LNG sources while building sovereign reinsurance capacity. The trap is treating this as a price spike rather than a structural reassessment of supply chain fragility. The IMF has already warned that prolonged disruption could push the global economy close to recession.

sections.heading: Wall Street's War Calculation: Trading Gains Mask Deeper Dealmaking Weakness
sections.body:
The Iran war has produced short-term trading gains for major banks — Goldman Sachs reported a 48% increase in investment banking fees, driven by volatility and energy-linked stocks — but market analysts warn that these mask underlying damage to Wall Street's core business model. The structural concern is that uncertainty over baseline conditions makes contract-writing nearly impossible.

US-announced mergers fell roughly 23% through early March. IPO activity slowed in March, with seven of the ten largest quarterly listings falling below offer price within a month. Banks with large trade finance operations — Citigroup, HSBC, Standard Chartered — have flagged rising counterparty risks in commodity-linked deals. The Dallas Fed described the conflict as the largest geopolitical oil supply shock on record, estimating that removing nearly one-fifth of global supply could cut global GDP growth by 2.9 percentage points in a single quarter.

Energy costs rose 10.9% in March, pushing US inflation to 3.3% — well above the Federal Reserve's target — while petrol prices climbed above $4 per gallon. The structural question is whether Wall Street's short-term trading profits are coming at the expense of long-term dealmaking capacity that underlies American financial power.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for financial institutions would be pushing for rapid conflict resolution rather than enduring short-term volatility profits. The trap is believing that trading gains during crisis reflect strength rather than fragility — when profitability falls, companies' willingness to pursue deals or borrow collapses, and Wall Street's fortunes are ultimately tied to Main Street's. Prolonged uncertainty is an existential threat to the dealmaking model, not a temporary headwind.

bottomLine: The Hormuz closure has revealed a structural vulnerability in the global energy system: chokepoints can be weaponized not through physical control but through insurance markets. Iran demonstrated that a $10,000 drone and a threat assessment can close a waterway carrying 20% of global oil and LNG. Russia has studied this playbook and has the capability to replicate it in European waters — the Baltic approaches for LNG, the Black Sea for grain. The insurance cascade operates faster than diplomatic consensus and below the threshold that triggers collective defense. The decisive move for the West is building state-backed reinsurance and permanent monitoring cells before the next crisis. The trap is believing that naval presence alone guarantees freedom of navigation — it does not. The market, not the navy, now decides which waterways are open.
sources: AP News, War on the Rocks, Foreign Affairs, Middle East Eye, Daily Sabah, Kyiv Independent, Reuters, IEA, Bloomberg, Reuters, CNN, Lloyd's List Intelligence, Dallas Fed
